name: Correspondence Principle
description: The Correspondence Principle, often summarized as "As above, so below; as below, so above," is an ancient Hermetic concept suggesting that there are patterns and relationships that exist on all levels of reality, from the microcosm to the macrocosm. Proponents believe that understanding one level can provide insights into others. This principle is central to various esoteric traditions, including alchemy, astrology, and magic. It suggests a unified, interconnected universe where everything is a reflection of everything else. Supporters cite observations of similar structures and patterns in nature (e.g., branching patterns in trees resembling blood vessels) as evidence. They also point to the supposed parallels between celestial events and earthly affairs as support for astrological claims. Mainstream science largely dismisses the Correspondence Principle as lacking empirical evidence and scientific rigor. Critics argue that observed similarities are often superficial and that attributing causality based on perceived resemblance is a form of confirmation bias. The principle's reliance on analogy and symbolism, rather than testable hypotheses, places it outside the realm of scientific inquiry. Skeptics also point out that the principle is often used to justify unsubstantiated claims and pseudoscientific beliefs. The principle's vagueness makes it difficult to falsify, a key requirement for scientific theories. While proponents see the principle as a key to unlocking hidden knowledge about the universe, mainstream science views it as a philosophical concept with limited practical value.
subtitle: Microcosm reflects Macrocosm.

claims.claim: The structure of the atom mirrors the solar system.
claims.evidence: Electrons orbiting the nucleus are likened to planets orbiting the sun.
claims.criticism: This is a superficial analogy. The forces and dynamics involved are fundamentally different. Quantum mechanics governs atomic structure, while gravity governs planetary motion.

claims.claim: Human society reflects the organization of the human body.
claims.evidence: Different social roles (e.g., leaders, workers) are analogous to different organs and systems within the body.
claims.criticism: This is a metaphorical comparison, not a literal correspondence. Social structures are far more complex and adaptable than biological systems. Such analogies can be used to justify social hierarchies.

claims.evidence: Astrological charts are used to predict personality traits and life events based on planetary positions.
claims.criticism: Astrology lacks scientific validity. Numerous studies have failed to demonstrate any correlation between astrological predictions and real-world outcomes. The Barnum effect explains why people find astrological readings personally relevant.

keyFigures.name: Hermes Trismegistus
keyFigures.role: Legendary author of the Emerald Tablet, a key text expounding the principle.

keyFigures.name: Carl Sagan
keyFigures.role: Popular science communicator who cautioned against pseudoscience and unsubstantiated claims.

timeline.event: Emergence of Hermetic texts and philosophy.
Integration of Hermetic ideas into alchemy and esoteric traditions.
Revival of Hermeticism and its influence on art and science.
Continued interest in the Correspondence Principle within New Age and occult movements.

sources.author: Dean, Geoffrey
sources.work: Recent Advances in Natal Astrology: A Critical Review, 1900-1976
sources.title: Dean, Geoffrey
